Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty structured but also kind of monotonous. I'd block out about four to five hours a day, usually in the afternoon and evening, and I'd go through the video lectures and try to take visual notes with diagrams and color-coding. I'm a visual learner so I'd spend a lot of time making mind maps and charts. Sometimes it felt like I was just going through the motions though, especially when the material wasn't grabbing me.
Q2: I tried to keep things regular because I know myself—if I don't have a schedule, everything falls apart. Most weeks I'd stick to my afternoon study blocks, but when deadlines hit, I'd cram way more hours in. The consistency helped me stay afloat even when I was feeling pretty bored or unmotivated with the content.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if new content was posted or seeing if there were any announcements about assignments. Sometimes I'd log in just to see discussion posts because that was more interesting than rewatching lectures. Deadlines were the biggest motivator though—nothing gets you logging in like realizing something's due in 48 hours.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks it just became automatic. I'd wake up, have breakfast, and check the LMS like checking social media. It wasn't always productive—sometimes I'd just scroll through and not really do anything meaningful. But having it as part of my routine meant I at least stayed connected to what was happening in the course.
Q5: The video lectures were essential for me because I need to see things explained visually. I also found the discussion boards surprisingly helpful—seeing how other students interpreted concepts gave me different perspectives. When someone posted a diagram or visual explanation, that was gold for me. Those peer interactions made the dry material more bearable.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials, to be honest. Long PDFs and text-heavy resources just don't work for my brain. I'd skim them at best or ignore them entirely. If there wasn't a visual component or a video explaining the same thing, I'd often just skip it and hope the exam wouldn't focus too heavily on that content.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to the assignments or exams. Also, if it was engaging—like interactive content or discussions where people actually responded. When something felt like busywork or just checking a box, I'd lose interest fast. I need to feel like I'm actually learning something, not just completing tasks.
Q8: Optional activities felt like they existed in a gray zone for me. I'd look at them and think 'this could be useful' but then the boredom would kick in and I'd convince myself I didn't have time. Required activities at least had the pressure to get done. Without that external push, my low motivation made it easy to skip optional stuff.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download lecture slides and videos and then study from my tablet offline. I made tons of handwritten visual notes, drew diagrams, created flashcards with images. I also used YouTube for alternative explanations when the course material wasn't clicking. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were whole study sessions where I'd be working from printed notes or my own mind maps spread across my desk. I'd spend hours connecting concepts visually on paper, testing myself with my own diagrams. The LMS would show nothing for those hours. Some of my best studying happened completely offline with materials I'd already downloaded.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download the slides, and then never go back to that video even though I'd review those slides dozens of times. My offline review was way more extensive than my online activity suggested. I preferred having everything saved locally so I could study without needing internet.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. The stress was really getting to me and I felt constantly bored with the material even though I knew I needed to engage with it. There were weeks where everything felt overwhelming and pointless at the same time. The mindfulness exercises they introduced actually helped me push through those moments, but it was a struggle.
Q13: Definitely. When I was stressed and had multiple things competing for my attention, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS—just enough to not fall behind. Fatigue made it hard to focus on videos, so I'd zone out even while technically watching them. The high stress combined with feeling bored made engagement feel like pulling teeth sometimes.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have a lecture video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be thinking about other problems or just feeling too bored to actually process what was being said. I'd technically log the watch time, but I'd have to go back later because nothing stuck the first time.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they were important. Part of it was the boredom—I just couldn't make myself care enough to start. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to begin. My assignment completion rate shows that struggle. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow and then tomorrow would come with the same excuses.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. Without them, I'd drift. When a deadline approached, suddenly I'd be logging in constantly, watching videos at 1.5x speed, posting in discussions to meet participation requirements. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically around due dates. It wasn't healthy but it's how I operated.
Q17: When I got feedback that was actually constructive, it motivated me to engage more with similar content. But generic feedback or just a grade with no explanation? That did nothing for me. I needed to understand what I was doing wrong visually—like marked-up examples. Good feedback made me want to improve; bad feedback made me feel like why bother.
Q18: The course structure was okay but pretty rigid. Having 15 online courses meant I was constantly juggling different structures and expectations. This course's weekly module format helped me stay organized, but sometimes the pacing felt off—too slow when I was engaged, too fast when I was struggling. More flexibility would have helped.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it matched my learning style. But the heavy reliance on reading materials made it harder. Also, when discussion prompts were vague or uninteresting, I'd just post something generic to meet requirements. Clearer visual guides and more interactive elements would have helped me stay engaged.
Q20: I think that happens when someone is trying to understand material but struggling to actually produce work from it. You might watch every video, read every post, genuinely try to learn—but when it comes to assignments, you freeze up or run out of time. High activity doesn't always mean high comprehension or confidence to submit work.
Q21: That's kind of me in some ways. My LMS activity was decent but a lot of my real learning happened offline with my own notes and diagrams. Someone could look at my logs and think I wasn't doing much during certain periods, but I was actually deep in study mode—just not in a way the system could track.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But they can't show if you're actually focused or just have a video playing in the background. They miss all the offline work, the thinking, the visual notes, the conversations with classmates outside the platform. They capture presence, not understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or logging in—it's being present mentally, making connections, wanting to know more. For me, engagement feels like when a concept clicks and I want to explore it further, not when I'm just checking boxes.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on visual learning strategies outside the platform, or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped me manage my stress enough to keep going. It wouldn't capture the internal battle between boredom and knowing I needed to push through. My resilience story isn't in the data—it's in the fact that I passed despite everything working against my motivation.
